stocks.note: Numbers as of market close April 2nd. Markets were closed April 3rd (Good Friday).
stocks.news:
Airlines are feeling the heat from surging fuel costs. United said it burned through $400 million more than expected last month and is raising checked bag fees by $10 to cushion the blow. Delta reports earnings this week — expect turbulence in its outlook too.

On the trade front, Trump rolled out pharma tariffs: zero for foreign drugmakers who manufacture in the U.S., 100% for everyone else — with a 120-day window to comply. Wall Street expected worse. Novo Nordisk, GSK, and AstraZeneca all popped over 1%.

Bright spot: March jobs crushed expectations — 178,000 added vs. the 59,000 estimate, and unemployment ticked down to 4.3%. But wage growth slipped to 3.5%, the lowest since 2021. Employers are hiring — just not paying more for it.

economy.commentary:
Oil remains the story. Brent crude hit $141 a barrel last week — its highest since 2008 — as the Strait of Hormuz blockade continues to choke 20% of global supply. Ship traffic through the strait has fallen from 150 per day to under 20. Gas is now $4.08 nationally, up $1.10 since the war started. Bay Area drivers? Still north of $6.

The strong jobs report adds a wrinkle: great news for employment, but it gives the Fed less reason to cut rates anytime soon. Gold pulled back slightly to $4,672 as investors weigh war uncertainty against economic resilience.

The 10-year yield dipped to 4.31% — bond markets pricing in slower wage growth despite the hiring beat.

crypto.commentary:
Crypto ticked higher — BTC rose to $67,505 and ETH held near $2,065, while XRP slipped to $1.30.

The bigger story is institutional adoption. Charles Schwab is launching direct Bitcoin and Ether trading for its 46 million clients in H1 2026. Morgan Stanley is prepping crypto through E*Trade. When firms managing $12 trillion start putting crypto on the same menu as stocks and bonds, it's no longer the fringe — it's the appetizer.

Coinbase also got conditional approval for digital asset custody, joining Ripple and Circle. The exchange said it won't offer banking services like deposits or lending — for now. Baby steps toward legitimacy.

real_estate.homebuilder: Strong jobs numbers and surging oil are keeping the Fed on hold — and mortgage rates stuck near 6.5% heading into peak spring buying season.
real_estate.commentary:
Total inventory across our 16-city tracker fell another 21 units to 2,501 — the fifth straight week of decline. But new listings rebounded to 176 (from 156 last week), and coming-soon jumped to 261 (from 250). The pipeline is filling, even as total supply keeps tightening.

📍 Union City: Biggest percentage drop — down 7 to just 82 total. One of the tightest markets in the tracker and getting tighter.

📍 Fremont: Ticked up 2 to 296. New listings jumped to 27 (from 19), while coming-soon pulled back to 54 (from 59). Sellers are starting their spring move.

📍 Milpitas: Up 3 to 116, but here's the stat of the week — only 1 new listing across the entire city. Inventory is building from pending conversions, not new sellers.

📍 Oakland: Down 4 to 732 with 42 new listings. Buyers continue to absorb faster than sellers can list.

📍 Dublin: Up 3 to 151 — slow and steady build continues.

📍 Newark: Flat at 101, holding near the century mark for the second week.

On the national front, private credit loan defaults jumped to 9.2% — more than triple the historical 2-2.5% norm — as AI startups eat into the software companies these funds bet on. Meanwhile, big investment firms are pivoting to warehouses and apartments for stable income and cutting office and retail bets, where deal volumes fell 46% last quarter. If more businesses default, the ripple could hit jobs, pensions, and retirement funds.
